attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of progressive fatigue, eight kilogram weight loss, and a new dry cough. He has a 40 pack-year smoking history but quit five years ago. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He worked as a carpenter but is now retired.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 128/78 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. There are reduced breath sounds in the right upper zone. A chest X-ray reveals a 6 cm peripheral mass in the right upper lobe. CT scan confirms the mass and shows multiple liver lesions. A CT-guided biopsy of the lung mass reveals poorly differentiated non-small cell carcinoma with large cells, abundant cytoplasm, and no evidence of squamous or glandular differentiation.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with clinical features suggestive of lung cancer, including constitutional symptoms, smoking history, and imaging showing a peripheral mass with metastatic disease. The biopsy findings of poorly differentiated non-small cell carcinoma without specific differentiation are characteristic of large cell carcinoma.

Adenocarcinoma typically shows glandular differentiation on histology and often stains positive for TTF-1 and napsin A. The absence of these features makes this diagnosis less likely.

Squamous cell carcinoma would show evidence of squamous differentiation including keratin pearls or intercellular bridges, which are not described in this case. It also tends to be more central in location.

Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma would show neuroendocrine features on immunohistochemistry, which are not described in this case. It has a worse prognosis than conventional large cell carcinoma.

Small cell carcinoma shows characteristic small cells with scant cytoplasm and nuclear molding, which is distinctly different from the large cells with abundant cytoplasm described in this case.

Large cell carcinoma represents about 3% of lung cancers and is a diagnosis of exclusion when a non-small cell lung cancer shows no clear squamous or glandular differentiation. It typically presents as a large peripheral mass and has a high propensity for early distant metastases. Treatment follows standard protocols for non-small cell lung cancer, with systemic therapy being the mainstay for metastatic disease.
learningPoints: Large cell carcinoma of the lung typically presents as a peripheral mass with early distant metastases.
Large cell carcinoma is a diagnosis of exclusion after ruling out other non-small cell lung cancer subtypes.
Treatment approach for large cell carcinoma follows non-small cell lung cancer protocols, with surgery for early-stage disease and systemic therapy for advanced disease.
